Name: adaptive_quantization_compression
Title: Adaptive Quantization Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach to Model Size Optimization
Short Hypothesis: This research posits that an adaptive quantization strategy, informed by rate-distortion theory, can optimize the bit allocation for model parameters such that the most task-relevant information is preserved while minimizing model size. This approach challenges the current heuristic methods that do not consider the information-theoretic limits on preserving task performance during quantization.
Related Work: Existing works have approached model compression through various techniques such as mixed-precision quantization (e.g., AMP-ViT, SNIP) and training-free proxies (EMQ). However, many of these methods do not explicitly utilize a rate-distortion framework to guide the quantization process or consider the specific mutual information between model parameters and task performance. Our proposal distinguishes itself by integrating a systematic, information-theoretic analysis into the adaptive quantization process, aiming to derive operational bounds and achievable rates.
Abstract: The increasing demand for deploying machine learning models in resource-constrained environments necessitates efficient model compression techniques. This proposal introduces 'Adaptive Quantization Compression,' a novel framework that applies rate-distortion theory to optimize the quantization of model parameters. By leveraging mutual information as a criterion for bit allocation, we aim to minimize the model size while ensuring the preservation of task-relevant information. Our method involves the derivation of a rate-distortion function specific to the model architecture and task at hand, allowing for an adaptive quantization strategy that dynamically allocates bits based on the sensitivity of model performance to distortion. We will validate our approach through experiments on benchmark datasets, comparing our method against existing quantization techniques in terms of accuracy, model size, and computational efficiency.
Experiments: 1. Theoretical Analysis: Derive the rate-distortion function for various model architectures (e.g., CNNs, Transformers) and tasks (e.g., image classification, language processing). 2. Adaptive Quantization Implementation: Develop an adaptive quantization algorithm that utilizes the derived rate-distortion functions to allocate bits dynamically during the quantization process. 3. Benchmarking: Evaluate the performance of our method against state-of-the-art quantization techniques using metrics such as model accuracy, size reduction, and computational efficiency across multiple datasets. 4. Sensitivity Analysis: Conduct experiments to assess the impact of varying bit allocations on model performance to identify optimal configurations.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of deriving accurate rate-distortion functions for various architectures and tasks may present challenges in practical implementation. The effectiveness of the adaptive quantization approach might vary across different model types, requiring extensive experimentation to establish generalizability. There may be limitations in maintaining performance when significantly reducing model size, depending on the nature of the task and data.
